macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Three Approaches to Land Division: Yotzei Mitzrayim, Ba'ei Ha'aretz, and Combined

micro_segments.title: Explanation of Yotzei Mitzrayim approach and Ba'ei Ha'aretz approach
Combined approach: both Yotzei Mitzrayim and Ba'ei Ha'aretz

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis Using Biblical Narratives: Bnei Yosef Complaint and the Problem of Tfelim

micro_segments.title: How the Yotzei Mitzrayim approach explains Bnei Yosef's complaint about insufficient land
Challenge to Ba'ei Ha'aretz: if proportional to population at entry, why would they complain?
Gemara's explanation via Tfelim: young people under 20 without living parents
Alternative explanation: equal tribal lands despite unequal populations

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Inference: No One Was Excluded from the Land (Rebutted)

micro_segments.title: Abaye's logic: if anyone was excluded, they would have complained and been recorded
Counterargument: Bnei Yosef complained but it didn't help; Torah writes complaints that succeeded morally
Alternative explanation: Torah records Bnei Yosef story for moral lesson about Ayin Hara, not historical completeness

macro_segments.title: Meraglim, Mitlonenim, and Adat Korach: Who Did Not Receive a Portion

micro_segments.title: Meraglim and the spies: Yehoshua and Kalev received their portion from the spies' land
Derasha on Chayu: they lived in their portion (inherited from the spies)
Mitlonenim and Adat Korach: two views on whether their portions went to Yehoshua and Kalev
Bnot Tzelofchad's argument: our father was not among the Meraglim, Mitlonenim, or Adat Korach

macro_segments.title: Bnot Tzelofchad and the Ten Portions of Menashe: Yotzei Mitzrayim vs Ba'ei Ha'aretz

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's question: why does the verse say Menashe got ten portions, not six?
Under Yotzei Mitzrayim: Tzelofchad gets his own portion plus Chefer's double portion as bechor plus two dead uncles' portions
Under Ba'ei Ha'aretz: Tzelofchad only inherits through Chefer (no own portion), gets two portions as bechor only
Gemara's resolution: both views posit dead uncles; the ten includes six Batei Avot plus four Bnot Tzelofchad portions

macro_segments.title: Braita on Inheritance Rights: Naton Titein and the Bechor Portion

micro_segments.title: Yotzei Mitzrayim approach: Naton Titein (direct inheritance), betoch achei avihen (with uncles), double portion as bechor
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: they inherited even the portion of a dead uncle (Chelek achi avihen)
How to count ten portions: accounting for two dead brothers' portions distributed to survivors
Rav Papa's question: what type of portions does the verse discuss—subdivisions or Batei Avot?
Gemara resolves: Batei Avot (major divisions) are the counting; Bnot Tzelofchad included to show they got a bechor portion

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Muchzeket: Eretz Yisrael as Inherited and Possessed Property

micro_segments.title: Question: how can Tzelofchad get a Chelek Bechor when the land was not in his father Chefer's actual possession?
Gemara's answer: Eretz Yisrael is viewed as Muchzeket (possessed/held), not merely Ra'uy (entitled to)

macro_segments.title: Inheritance of Children: Through Paternal or Maternal Grandfathers

micro_segments.title: Braita: children of excluded groups inherited through Avi Avihem and Avi Imoteihem (paternal and maternal grandfathers)
Gemara resolves apparent contradiction: either Yotzei Mitzrayim view (through grandfathers) or Ba'ei Ha'aretz view (some children got own portion)
Alternative: both assume Ba'ei Ha'aretz but distinguish by age—under 20 vs. age 20 and above

topical_tags.term: Yotzei Mitzrayim (those who left Egypt)
Ba'ei Ha'aretz (those who entered the land)
Bnei Yosef (tribe of Joseph/Ephraim and Menashe)
Tfelim (young people under 20)
Abaye's inference: no exclusion without complaint
Meraglim (spies who scouted the land)
Chayu (they lived/were enriched): interpretation of survival
Adat Korach (Korach's congregation)
Mitlonenim (complainers against God, Korach's followers)
Bnot Tzelofchad (daughters of Tzelofchad)
Tzelofchad (father excluded from land; his case and lineage)
Chefer/Hefer (brother of Tzelofchad, had daughters)
Yehoshua and Kalev (spies who did not rebel)
Ayin Hara (evil eye; why Bnei Yosef should hide their size)
Menashe (tribe; six sons as heads of Batei Avot)
Batei Avot (father's houses; major tribal divisions)
Chelek Bechor (double portion of firstborn)
Naton Titein (give give—direct inheritance)
Achuzat Nachalah (possession/holding of inheritance)
Muchzeket (possessed; held as if in possession)
Ra'uy (entitled to but not in possession)
Avi Avihem v'Avi Imoteihem (paternal and maternal grandfathers)
Zchut Atzman (merit of one's own)
